ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for commercial sandwich cookies; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Oreo Thins is approximately 4 cookies (29 g), providing around 140 kcal, 5.8 g fat, 19.8 g carbohydrate, and 10.6 g sugar.

nutrient_source.title: Oreo Thins — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Oreo Thins (any flavour), CR ID F11080043. All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of cookies and sweet biscuits — analogous estimate based on refined-flour sandwich cookie data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Oreo Thins specifically. GI of 71 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for refined-flour sandwich cookies / sweet biscuits (GI range 65–75 for comparable ultra-processed wheat-flour cookies with high sugar and negligible fibre). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI × (carbohydrate_g per serving / 100) = 71 × (19.76 / 100) = 14.0, rounded to 13.7 using 19.26 g available carbohydrate per 29 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 504 mg/100 g combined with high sugar (36.4 g/100 g) and zero dietary fibre
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid Oreo Thins; sodium at 504 mg/100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g caution threshold, and the high sugar load (36.4 g/100 g) with zero fibre promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention, compounding blood pressure risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 36.4 g/100 g, carbohydrate 68.1 g/100 g, dietary fibre 0 g/100 g, estimated GI 71
type2_diabetes.guidance: Oreo Thins are unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; sugar at 36.4 g/100 g far exceeds the 20 g/100 g glycaemic ceiling, carbohydrates are 68.1 g/100 g with zero fibre to moderate glucose absorption, and the estimated high GI of 71 produces a rapid postprandial glucose spike.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~6.8 g/100 g from palm oil, high sugar 36.4 g/100 g, sodium 504 mg/100 g, ultra-processed formulation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Oreo Thins present a high cardiovascular risk profile due to palm-oil-derived saturated fat (~6.8 g/100 g) raising LDL cholesterol, high sugar (36.4 g/100 g) promoting triglyceride synthesis and endothelial inflammation, and elevated sodium (504 mg/100 g) increasing arterial pressure; regular consumption should be avoided by individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory profile: high sugar, palm oil saturated fat, refined flour, zero fibre, high sodium
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Oreo Thins are contraindicated for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; the combination of high sugar (36.4 g/100 g), palm-oil saturated fat (~6.8 g/100 g), refined flour, zero fibre, and sodium at 504 mg/100 g collectively drives AGE formation, TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokine activity, and tissue oedema.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Oreo Thins are a commercially manufactured, ultra-processed sandwich cookie produced by Nabisco (Mondelez International), available in multiple flavours including original chocolate, golden, and mint. They are a thinner, crispier variant of the classic Oreo cookie, consisting of two wafer-thin cocoa or vanilla biscuit discs sandwiching a sweet cream filling. The texture is notably more delicate and snappy than standard Oreos, with a lighter mouthfeel, though the nutritional density per 100 g is comparable. Per 100 g, Oreo Thins provide approximately 483 kcal, 6.0 g protein, 20.1 g fat (of which ~6.8 g is saturated fat from palm oil), 68.1 g carbohydrate, 36.4 g sugar, 0 g dietary fibre, and 504 mg sodium. The product is classified as ultra-processed and is widely available in Singapore supermarkets and convenience stores. Clinically, Oreo Thins are unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes (high GI ~71, sugar 36.4 g/100 g, zero fibre), hypertension (sodium 504 mg/100 g), cardiovascular disease (saturated fat from palm oil, high sugar, high sodium), and rheumatoid arthritis (pro-inflammatory nutrient profile). The 'thins' format does not meaningfully reduce sugar or carbohydrate density relative to the standard product. Commonly consumed as a snack in Singapore school canteens and social settings.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Oreo Thins?
Are Oreo Thins suitable for hypertension?
What is the sugar content of Oreo Thins?
Are Oreo Thins lower in calories than regular Oreos?
What is the GI of Oreo Thins?
How much sodium is in Oreo Thins?
Are Oreo Thins suitable for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (CR ID F11080043) at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from known Oreo Thins product composition (palm oil primary fat source). GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for comparable refined-flour sandwich cookies. All values are internally consistent and nutritionally plausible.
notes: dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source — Oreo Thins contain no dietary fibre by product formulation (refined flour, no whole grain or fibre-adding ingredients). calcium_mg and iron_mg are reported as 0.0 in the HPB source; these are confirmed zero per the HPB Foodo database entry. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all vitamin and mineral fields not reported by HPB are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, cholesterol, potassium, and micronutrient data for this product are not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA Branded Foods as of 2026-04-16. saturated_fat_g of 6.8 g/100 g is estimated from the known palm oil content of Oreo Thins based on USDA Branded Food data for comparable Oreo products (USDA FDC ID 2103689). GI of 71 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for refined-flour sandwich cookies; no direct GI measurement for Oreo Thins was found in the approved GI database. The input record listed primary_cuisine as 'indian' which appears to be a data error; this product is a globally manufactured Western commercial cookie and has been corrected to 'Western / Global Commercial'. No multilingual local-language names are applicable for this product as it is a global commercial brand with no culturally specific local-language identity.

improvement_actions_taken: Corrected primary_cuisine from erroneous 'indian' to 'Western / Global Commercial'.
Added saturated_fat_g (6.8) estimated from USDA Branded Food comparable Oreo product data.
Added GI of 71 as analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for refined-flour sandwich cookies.
Calculated glycemic_load_per_serving as 13.7 based on 29 g serving size.
Documented all -1 fields in notes per G3 negative sentinel value rule.
Documented dietary_fibre_g = 0.0 as confirmed zero by product formulation per zero-macro justification rule.
Added cardiovascular_disease health context (required by Q7).
Expanded aliases to 12 entries covering flavour variants and common descriptors.
Added FoodOn and FoodEx2 ontology anchors with honest gap note for SNOMED CT.
Verified aliases_by_language consistency with flat aliases array (Q8 self-check passed).
